skill.name: codeconscious-identity
skill.description: Defines the CodeConscious runtime identity, command workflows, memory practices, planning methods, and reflection routines for AI coding assistants.
skill.summary: Use CodeConscious runtime workflows for structured code exploration, planning, memory, and self-review.
skill.author: Dwsy

security_audit.summary: AI review confirmed that most static command detections are Markdown command examples or inline command names, not hidden executable payloads. The skill still contains high-risk guidance: a copied task executor runs task-provided shell commands with shell=True and writes user-provided paths, while the docs encourage broad filesystem exploration and adjacent memory access. No prompt injection attempt or credential exfiltration was found, so this is high risk but not a confirmed malicious block.

high_findings.title: Documented Task Executor Runs Untrusted Shell Commands
high_findings.description: The auxiliary task executor dispatches task objects with type "command" directly into _run_command, and _run_command calls subprocess.run with shell=True. If users copy this pattern into an agent workflow, task-controlled strings can become command injection or arbitrary command execution.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is direct and semantically confirmed: task["command"] flows to subprocess.run with shell=True. It is documentation rather than installed code, but it is presented as a reusable helper script.

high_findings.title: Path-Controlled File Writes in Execution Helper
high_findings.description: The same helper creates files using task-provided file_path values without path validation or workspace containment checks. A copied implementation could overwrite sensitive project files or write outside the intended workspace.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write call is explicit and uses the task-supplied path. The risk depends on whether a user turns the Markdown example into executable automation.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Exploration and Temporary File Use
medium_findings.description: The exploration workflow shows a shell script that scans the current tree, excludes common directories, and writes file inventory and package metadata into /tmp. This is expected for a code exploration skill, but it can expose project structure or create shared temporary files if used without containment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and intentional. The behavior is legitimate for code analysis, but the temporary paths and broad scan increase operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Adjacent Runtime and Memory Directory Access
medium_findings.description: The skill points users to adjacent constitution, memory, cognition, and toolkit directories using parent-directory paths. This explains the path traversal flags; it is not malicious by itself, but marketplace users should know the skill expects access beyond its own folder.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory references are explicit. They appear to describe expected companion runtime folders, not an attempt to evade review.

medium_findings.title: Executable Shell Examples Are Common Across Documentation
medium_findings.description: Many static external-command detections are Markdown examples for runtime commands, Python snippets, grep, psql, npm, and memory CLI usage. Most are not hidden execution, but the skill is command-heavy and should be treated as operational guidance rather than passive text.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static hits are real command examples in Markdown. The risk is moderate because they are intended user workflows, not code that executes on install.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs found during static analysis are a badge URL, a GitHub documentation link, and a JSON Schema identifier. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references. I found no network client code, webhook destination, or credential transfer tied to them.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Database Detection Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The critical sensitive-file hit points to an example prompt about implementing SAML authentication. This is not a Windows SAM database path or credential extraction instruction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains the term SAML in an authentication planning example. It does not reference the Windows SAM file or any local credential database.

low_findings.title: High Entropy and Weak Crypto Flags Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-entropy findings appear to come from Chinese-language Markdown and dense documentation, not encoded payloads. Weak-crypto detections align with Markdown, planning examples, or words such as design and medium, not cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are readable Markdown, not binary or encrypted content. The cited weak-crypto lines do not implement hashing or encryption.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run With shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: A reusable helper script shows subprocess.run(command, shell=True) with command text sourced from task data. This is the main dangerous pattern confirmed by AI review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact dangerous API and shell=True option are present. The surrounding code confirms task-provided strings can reach it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Construction With Unvalidated File Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper builds psql and npm commands from sql_file and test_files values before sending them to the shell runner. Unvalidated file names can alter shell behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command construction is explicit. The injection impact is high if task fields are controlled by user input or model output.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated Path Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper writes content to Path(file_path) without checking whether the path remains inside a trusted workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path is used directly. The finding is limited by the fact that this is an example in Markdown, not a packaged executable file.

content.value_statement: Complex coding work often loses context across exploration, planning, and implementation. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured runtime identity with memory, planning, reflection, and command workflows.
actual_capabilities: Defines runtime modes for exploration, learning, thinking, planning, iteration, memory, and reflection.
Guides progressive loading of reference documents for commands, constitution rules, response style, and self-assessment.
Provides examples for codebase exploration, dependency mapping, and implementation planning.
Describes short-term, long-term, and episodic memory practices for project knowledge.
Includes quality review rubrics for confidence, reasoning, and continuous improvement.
limitations: Most functionality is Markdown guidance, not packaged executable software.
Some workflows assume adjacent memory, cognition, runtime, and toolkit folders exist.
Command examples may require local tools such as Python, Node.js, psql, grep, fzf, or ripgrep.
Shell automation examples require security review before use in an agent workflow.

use_cases.title: Map an unfamiliar repository
use_cases.description: Use the exploration workflow to summarize project structure, dependencies, hot spots, and follow-up questions.

use_cases.title: Plan a multi-step feature
use_cases.description: Use the planning workflow to break requirements into tasks, dependencies, risks, and validation checkpoints.

use_cases.title: Preserve project knowledge
use_cases.description: Use memory and reflection guidance to record decisions, incidents, learned patterns, and future improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Explore a repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the CodeConscious exploration workflow to inspect this repository in read-only mode. Summarize architecture, important files, risks, and questions.

prompt_templates.title: Think through a problem
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the CodeConscious thinking workflow for this issue. Explain your assumptions, evidence, likely root causes, confidence, and next checks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan implementation work
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the CodeConscious planning workflow for this requirement. Produce tasks, dependencies, risks, acceptance checks, and a practical execution order.

prompt_templates.title: Review runtime automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced security review before using examples
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the CodeConscious command and file automation examples before implementation. Identify unsafe shell, path, network, and memory access patterns.

output_examples.output: A concise repository map with major modules, dependency relationships, likely ownership areas, risks, and recommended next files to inspect.

output_examples.input: Plan implementation for password reset and token expiry.
output_examples.output: A sequenced task plan covering data changes, service logic, API behavior, security checks, tests, rollout risks, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Reflect on the last debugging session.
output_examples.output: A self-review covering what worked, what evidence was weak, what memory should be saved, and what process should improve next time.

best_practices: Use read-only exploration before planning changes or running commands.
Treat every shell and file-write example as pseudocode until reviewed and sandboxed.
Record durable decisions and lessons only after validating them against project evidence.
anti_patterns: Do not copy the shell executor into production without argument validation and workspace containment.
Do not let model-generated task fields decide arbitrary command strings or file paths.
Do not store secrets, credentials, or private user data in memory files.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It defines CodeConscious runtime workflows for code exploration, planning, implementation support, memory, and reflection.

faq.question: Does it install executable tools?
faq.answer: No. The package is mostly Markdown guidance and examples. Some examples describe commands that users may run manually.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The documentation includes a reusable helper that runs task-provided shell commands and writes task-provided paths.

faq.question: Did the audit find data exfiltration?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The detected URLs are documentation and badge references, not outbound data transfer code.

faq.question: How should users apply it safely?
faq.answer: Use it as structured guidance, keep exploration read-only first, and review all command automation before execution.
